macro_segments.title: The Mishna's Apparent Redundancy: Why State Both the Eleventh and Twelfth Year Boundaries?

micro_segments.title: Initial Challenge: If Vows Are Valid at Twelve and a Day, Why Mention Eleven and a Day?
First Resolution: The Mishna Must Be Teaching That Checking Occurs Throughout the Entire Twelfth Year
Second Resolution: Both Boundaries Necessary to Establish the Precise Windows for Checking and Validity
Addressing Further Redundancy: Why Restate Boundaries When Their Logical Consequences Are Implied?
Final Clarification: Explicit Boundaries Prevent Misreading and Establish That These Are Inviolable Rules, Not Subject to Contrary Claims

macro_segments.title: Why Girls Mature at Twelve and Boys at Thirteen: Divine Wisdom and Educational Factors

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon ben Elazar's Reversed Position: Girls at Thirteen, Boys at Twelve—Why the Minority View?
Rabbi's Position: God Granted Women Superior Understanding via the Word Vayiven, Explaining Earlier Maturity
Educational Explanation: Boys Achieve Maturity Earlier Because They Attend School More Regularly Than Girls
Tosafot's Clarification: Even Without a Formal Teacher, Boys Mature Faster by Association with School Peers

macro_segments.title: When Does the Year Before Bar/Bat Mitzvah Count: Debate Over Status Before Adulthood

micro_segments.title: The Question Framed: Does the Year Leading Up Count Like the Time Before or Like the Time After?
Vows Context: For Vows, the Year Before Is Neither Fully Before Nor Fully After—It's In-Between
Punishment Context: Rav and Rabbi Chanina Say the Year Before Is Like Before; Rabbi Yochanan and Rabbi Yehoshua Say It's Like After
Sign Reference (Vayizen): Extracting Position from Circumlocution—The Feminine Form Points to Rabbi Chanina's View
Rav Hamnuna's Challenge: After Twelve and a Day, Vows Are Binding Regardless of Knowledge—Does This Prove Pre-Mitzvah Doesn't Count?
The Connection to Pubic Hair: The Year Before Is Meaningless Because Pubic Hair Only Acquires Significance at the Age Boundary
Rav Zeira's Proof: Biblical Language Treat a Man as Adult Only at the Boundary; Before That, No Status
Conclusion: The Year Before Is Like the Period Before—Not Meaningful Until the Boundary is Crossed

macro_segments.title: The Braita on Pubic Hair: Ages Nine Through Thirteen and When Hairs Count as Signs of Maturity

micro_segments.title: Before Nine: Any Pubic Hair Is Shuma (Fatty Growth), Not Meaningful
Nine to Twelve: Hair Still Counts as Shuma—It's Random, Not True Pubic Hair
Rabbi Yosei's Dissent: Even from Nine, Pubic Hair Could Be a Sign of Maturity
Internal Contradiction in the Braita: Why State Twelve When You've Already Stated the Nine-to-Twelve Period?
Resolving the Contradiction: The Braita May Be Discussing Either Boys vs. Girls or Different Tannaim's Opinions
Rav Krispida's Qualification of Rabbi Yosei: Hair from Age Nine Counts Only If Still Present at Bar/Bat Mitzvah
Age as Independent Criterion: Hair Growth and Age Are Two Separate Requirements, Both Necessary

macro_segments.title: Meeun and Chalitzah: Testing Adulthood When Pubic Hair Status Is Unknown

micro_segments.title: Meeun Law: Before Bat Mitzvah, a Girl Can Refuse a Rabbinic Marriage; After, She Cannot
Chalitzah Law: A Girl Must Be Definitively Adult (Not a Minor) for Chalitzah to Be Valid
Rava's Position: Throughout the Twelfth Year, She Might Be an Adult or a Minor—No Meeun, No Chalitzah
Apparent Contradiction: How Can Rava Say No Meeun (Implying Adult) Yet Also Say No Chalitzah (Implying Minor)?
Rava's Assumption: Without Checking, You Can Assume Pubic Hair Is Present—But This Is Not an Absolute Certainty
Case Where Hairs Were Checked and Not Found: Doubt Remains Whether They Were There and Fell Out
Reconciling Rava: The Assumption Applies Only to Rabbinic Laws (Like Meeun), Not Biblical Laws (Like Chalitzah)
Concern About Fallen Hairs: Even If You Don't See Hairs, You Must Assume They Might Have Been Present and Fallen Out

macro_segments.title: Application to Marriage Consummation: Rabbinic vs. Biblical Consequences of Hair Uncertainty

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Rabbinic Marriage Before Bat Mitzvah, Consummation After—If No Hair Is Observed, Can She Do Meeun?
Concern: If She's Now Biblically Married (via Post-Mitzvah Relations), You Cannot Assume She's a Minor for Meeun Purposes
Scenario Without Consummation: If No Post-Mitzvah Relations, Marriage Remains Purely Rabbinic and She Can Assume Minorship

macro_segments.title: Vows by the Year-Before Minor: Is a Binding Vow Sufficient to Treat the Minor as an Adult for All Purposes?

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna's Claim: The Year Before, If You Make a Vow and Then Violate It, You Deserve Lashes
Biblical Source: The Word Yafli in 'Ish Ki Yafli' Includes the Year Before in Its Scope
Baraita Support: The Torah Treats a Minor Like an Adult for Violations of Oaths and Forbidden Objects
Clarification: The Prohibition Is 'Bal Yachel' (Rabbinic Issue of Invalidation), Not a Biblical Lashing Violation
Who Transgresses? The Minor Sanctifies; Adults Who Later Use the Object Transgress Bal Te'acher, Not the Minor
Kahana's Opinion: Even Adults Don't Get Lashes When a Minor Sanctifies—Truly a Rabbinic Enactment

Hekdesh (Sanctification / sacred status)

Rav Kahana (Opinion: no lashes for adult who eats sanctified object)
